title: Acute Uterine Inversion
chief_complaint: Just delivered vaginally. After placenta delivery, she suddenly has severe abdominal pain, massive vaginal bleeding, and she feels like something is coming out of her vagina.

physical_exam.Pelvic: Bluish-gray mass protruding from vagina (inverted fundus). Placenta attached to inverted fundus. Massive hemorrhage (estimated blood loss 2L).
physical_exam.General: Diaphoretic, cold, pale (Class III hemorrhagic shock).

hidden_data.cbc: Hb 5.5 (acute blood loss), Platelets 150,000, INR 2.0, Fibrinogen 180 (DIC developing)
hidden_data.bedside_us: Uterine fundus absent from normal position. Inverted uterus visible in vagina.

correct_diagnosis: Acute Uterine Inversion (Complete) with Massive Postpartum Hemorrhage and Hemorrhagic Shock - OBSTETRIC EMERGENCY
key_learning_points: Uterine inversion = fundus collapses into uterine cavity and protrudes through cervix. Rare (1:2,000-20,000 deliveries) but LIFE-THREATENING. Leading cause: Excessive fundal pressure + uterine atony + fundal placenta. Other causes: Cord traction before placental separation, Precipitous delivery, Connective tissue disorders

Classification: 1st degree (incomplete): Fundus inverted but above cervix. 2nd degree (complete): Fundus protrudes through cervix into vagina. 3rd degree (prolapsed): Fundus protrudes beyond introitus. 4th degree (total): Vagina and uterus completely inverted

Massive hemorrhage: Inversion causes severe pain → vagal response → hypotension + bradycardia. Hemorrhage from atonic uterus + disrupted placental bed. Shock often out of proportion to blood loss (neurogenic component from vagal stimulation)

IMMEDIATE MANAGEMENT: 1) Call for help (OB, Anesthesia, OR team). 2) DO NOT remove placenta if still attached (removes tamponade, increases hemorrhage). 3) MANUAL REPLACEMENT (Johnson method): Grasp fundus with palm, push through cervix toward umbilicus. Apply firm pressure at lead point (last part that inverted - usually fundus). 4) Once replaced: Keep hand in uterus until firm contraction. Start Oxytocin, Methergine, Hemabate (uterotonics). 5) If manual replacement fails: O'Sullivan hydrostatic technique (infuse 2L NS into vagina via IV tubing - distends vagina, pushes fundus back). 6) If still fails: EMERGENCY LAPAROTOMY + Huntington procedure (grasp round ligaments, pull fundus back through cervix) or Hautainer procedure (vertical incision of posterior cervical ring)

Replaced: Continue uterotonics (Oxytocin 40U in 1L NS at 250mL/hr, Methergine 0.2mg IM, Hemabate 250mcg IM q15min up to 8 doses). Broad-spectrum antibiotics (endometritis risk). Monitor for re-inversion. If DIC: MTP, replace clotting factors. Prognosis: Mortality <1% with prompt recognition and treatment
